name: Vancomycin
executiveSummary: Bactericidal glycopeptide against beta-lactam–resistant Gram-positive organisms (MRSA, enterococci, and others). High-risk medication: slow infusion (1 h), serum level monitoring, nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity.
indications:
## Main indications

- Treatment of infections caused by beta-lactam–resistant Gram-positive microorganisms (*MRSA, S. viridans, Bacillus, Corynebacterium*).
- Sepsis, bacteremia, meningitis, septic arthritis, endocarditis, osteomyelitis, nosocomial pneumonia due to *S. aureus*.
- Pseudomembranous colitis due to *Clostridioides difficile*: oral route according to severity.

## Precautions

- Administer by infusion over 1 hour; risk of red man syndrome with hypotension if infusion is rapid.
- Caution regarding necrosis with extravasation; monitor trough serum levels according to indication.
- Adjust dose in renal insufficiency per institutional protocol.

> Adjust according to institutional protocol and medical prescription.

adulto.presentation: Powder for injection 500–1000 mg (Rivervan, Vancomax, V. Fabra, V. Northia, V. Richet, Varedet).
adulto.reconstitution: 10 mL sterile water for injection. Conc: 100 mg/mL.
adulto.administration: Direct IV: No. Intermittent IV: Yes. Dilute 500 mg in 100 mL 0.9% NaCl and administer over no less than 60–90 min. Oral: Yes (*C. difficile* colitis).
adulto.notes: Plasma vancomycin monitoring (from 48 h of treatment). Trough according to indication: 15–20 µg/mL (bacteremia, endocarditis, osteomyelitis, meningitis, nosocomial MRSA pneumonia); 10–15 µg/mL for other indications.

pediatrico.reconstitution: Sterile water for injection for reconstitution.
pediatrico.dose: IV — Sepsis, bacteremia, meningitis, and septic arthritis: 60 mg/kg/day every 6 h. IV — Other infections: 40 mg/kg/day every 8 h. IV — Cystic fibrosis: 45 mg/kg/day every 8 h. Maximum dose: 2 g/day (up to 4 g/day if underdosed). PO — *C. difficile* colitis: 10 mg/kg/dose every 6 h (max. 125 mg/dose; severe or fulminant: max. 500 mg/dose), 10 days.
pediatrico.notes: High-risk medication. Ototoxic, nephrotoxic. May cause phlebitis, febrile reactions, rash, eosinophilia, neutropenia. Bolus administration may induce cardiac arrest.

neonatal.dose: Dose and interval according to postmenstrual age and renal function (NICU protocol).
neonatal.administration: Central route preferred; monitor access and signs of extravasation.

stability:
## Reconstituted (in vial)

- 24 h refrigerated.

## Diluted solution (for administration)

- 24 h at room temperature.

## Pediatric guide

- 96 h refrigerated.
adverseEffects:
## Common

- Red man syndrome with hypotension (associated with infusion rate).
- Phlebitis, febrile reactions, rash, eosinophilia, neutropenia.

## Serious

- Ototoxicity, nephrotoxicity.

bibliography.citation: Hospital de Pediatría SAMIC Prof. Dr. Juan P. Garrahan. Institutional Pharmaceutical Formulary — Vancomycin Hydrochloride (code 0216, ATC J01XA).
Infectious Diseases Service, Infection Prevention and Control. UCIP 2026 — Dilution and stability guide.
Rybak MJ, et al. Therapeutic monitoring of vancomycin: revised consensus guidelines. Am J Health Syst Pharm. 2020.
Sanford Guide to Antimicrobial Therapy — Vancomycin dosing and monitoring.
